title: Effect of annealing temperature on the microstructure and tensile properties of Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel
abstract_summary: The microstructure evolution and tensile properties of Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel annealed between 700°C and 1200°C for 1 h were investigated. Phases present include ferrite (α), austenite (γ) and κ-carbide (κ), with κ-carbide dissolving between 850–900°C. Below 900°C, increasing temperature raises austenite fraction and elongation but lowers strength, while above 900°C grain coarsening degrades both strength and ductility. Optimal properties (UTS 855 MPa, elongation 36.6%) are obtained after annealing at 900°C.
research_objective: To clarify the effect of annealing temperature on the microstructure evolution and tensile properties of a Mn-lean Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel, and to establish microstructure-property relationships governing strength and ductility.
main_process_variables: annealing temperature: 700–1200 °C, holding time: 1 h, water quenching
hot rolling finishing temperature: ~880 °C, water quench to ~300 °C

key_sections.purpose: Reviews low-density Fe-Mn-Al-C steels, emphasizes the need to study Mn-lean compositions, and states the research aim.
Details material preparation, thermomechanical processing, annealing treatments, characterization (XRD, OM, SEM, TEM), and tensile testing methods.
Presents equilibrium phase diagram, microstructure evolution (phase fractions, grain size, κ-carbide morphology), and tensile properties/fracture mechanisms across annealing temperatures.
Analyzes the effect of annealing temperature on microstructure and correlates phase fractions, grain size, and κ-carbide distribution with tensile properties.
Summarizes the dissolution temperature of κ-carbide, phase fraction trends, grain growth effects, and the optimal annealing condition (900°C) achieving best strength–ductility combination.
